source_paper_title: Efficient and Near-Optimal Online Portfolio Selection
source_paper_bibtex: @article{J_z_quel_2025, title={Efficient and Near-Optimal Online Portfolio Selection}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0175}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0175}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Jézéquel, Rémi and Ostrovskii, Dmitrii and Gaillard, Pierre}, year={2025}, month=may }
context:
Page 3, Introduction (end of discussion of Universal Portfolios and computational barriers).

Meanwhile, no algorithm except Universal Portfolios is known to admit a regret bound matching (5) up to a constant factor. In other words, the following problem stands:
Propose a computationally feasible and near-optimal online portfolio selection algorithm.
title: Design practical regret-optimal online portfolio selection algorithms without high-dimensional integration
background_latex:
Consider the online portfolio selection game over a horizon \(T\in\mathbb{N}\) with \(d\ge 2\) assets.

1) Decision set: the simplex \(\Delta_d:=\{w\in\mathbb{R}^d_+:\sum_{i=1}^d w[i]=1\}\).

2) Adversary’s move: at each round \(t\in\{1,\dots,T\}\), after observing the learner’s choice \(w_t\in\Delta_d\), the adversary reveals a nonnegative return vector \(x_t\in\mathbb{R}^d_+\).

3) Loss: the learner suffers the logarithmic loss
\[
\ell_t(w):=-\log(x_t^\top w),\qquad w\in\Delta_d.
\]

4) Comparator class: constantly rebalanced portfolios (CRP), i.e. fixed \(w\in\Delta_d\) used in every round.

5) Regret against the best CRP in hindsight:
\[
R_T(w_{1:T}\mid x_{1:T}) := \sum_{t=1}^T \ell_t(w_t)\; -\; \min_{w\in\Delta_d}\sum_{t=1}^T \ell_t(w).
\]

Cover’s Universal Portfolios strategy selects at time \(t\) a portfolio equal to the expectation under a density \(\phi_t\) on \(\Delta_d\) proportional to \(\exp\big(-\sum_{\tau=1}^{t-1} \ell_\tau(w)\big)\), which yields regret \((d-1)\log(T+1)\) for all sequences \(x_{1:T}\). However, this update requires (approximately) computing a \(d\)-dimensional integral (the expectation under \(\phi_t\)), which is computationally expensive.

An algorithm is called computationally feasible here if its per-round update can be implemented in time polynomial in \(d\) and \(T\) with reasonable dependence (e.g. nearly quadratic in \(d\)), and without requiring high-dimensional numerical integration or expensive sampling from \(\phi_t\) at each round.
problem_definition_latex:
Construct an online portfolio selection algorithm (a mapping from past observations to \(w_t\in\Delta_d\)) such that:

1) For every horizon \(T\) and every adversarial sequence \(x_{1:T}\in(\mathbb{R}^d_+)^T\), the regret satisfies
\[
R_T(w_{1:T}\mid x_{1:T}) = O\big(d\log T\big)
\]
(up to universal constant factors, matching the \(\Theta(d\log T)\) worst-case optimal rate of Universal Portfolios).

2) Each round’s update is computationally feasible in the sense of avoiding explicit high-dimensional integration over \(\Delta_d\) (as required by Universal Portfolios) and admitting an efficient implementation with polynomial-time per-round complexity in \(d\) and \(T\).
latex:
Consider the online portfolio selection game over a horizon \(T\in\mathbb{N}\) with \(d\ge 2\) assets.

1) Decision set: the simplex \(\Delta_d:=\{w\in\mathbb{R}^d_+:\sum_{i=1}^d w[i]=1\}\).

2) Adversary’s move: at each round \(t\in\{1,\dots,T\}\), after observing the learner’s choice \(w_t\in\Delta_d\), the adversary reveals a nonnegative return vector \(x_t\in\mathbb{R}^d_+\).

3) Loss: the learner suffers the logarithmic loss
\[
\ell_t(w):=-\log(x_t^\top w),\qquad w\in\Delta_d.
\]

4) Comparator class: constantly rebalanced portfolios (CRP), i.e. fixed \(w\in\Delta_d\) used in every round.

5) Regret against the best CRP in hindsight:
\[
R_T(w_{1:T}\mid x_{1:T}) := \sum_{t=1}^T \ell_t(w_t)\; -\; \min_{w\in\Delta_d}\sum_{t=1}^T \ell_t(w).
\]

Cover’s Universal Portfolios strategy selects at time \(t\) a portfolio equal to the expectation under a density \(\phi_t\) on \(\Delta_d\) proportional to \(\exp\big(-\sum_{\tau=1}^{t-1} \ell_\tau(w)\big)\), which yields regret \((d-1)\log(T+1)\) for all sequences \(x_{1:T}\). However, this update requires (approximately) computing a \(d\)-dimensional integral (the expectation under \(\phi_t\)), which is computationally expensive.

An algorithm is called computationally feasible here if its per-round update can be implemented in time polynomial in \(d\) and \(T\) with reasonable dependence (e.g. nearly quadratic in \(d\)), and without requiring high-dimensional numerical integration or expensive sampling from \(\phi_t\) at each round.

Construct an online portfolio selection algorithm (a mapping from past observations to \(w_t\in\Delta_d\)) such that:

1) For every horizon \(T\) and every adversarial sequence \(x_{1:T}\in(\mathbb{R}^d_+)^T\), the regret satisfies
\[
R_T(w_{1:T}\mid x_{1:T}) = O\big(d\log T\big)
\]
(up to universal constant factors, matching the \(\Theta(d\log T)\) worst-case optimal rate of Universal Portfolios).

2) Each round’s update is computationally feasible in the sense of avoiding explicit high-dimensional integration over \(\Delta_d\) (as required by Universal Portfolios) and admitting an efficient implementation with polynomial-time per-round complexity in \(d\) and \(T\).
location_in_paper: Page 3, Introduction (end of discussion of Universal Portfolios and computational barriers).
open_problem_quote:
Meanwhile, no algorithm except Universal Portfolios is known to admit a regret bound matching (5) up to a constant factor. In other words, the following problem stands:
Propose a computationally feasible and near-optimal online portfolio selection algorithm.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question is existential/constructive (design an algorithm) rather than a universal inequality that might be falsified by a finite counterexample instance search. Automated instance generation is unlikely to directly yield progress, since failure depends on worst-case regret over all sequences.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can parameterize candidate online algorithms (e.g., FTRL/OMD-style with tunable regularizers, update rules, and approximations) and score them via simulated regret on adversarially generated markets. However, the fitness signal (worst-case regret) is hard to approximate reliably, and proving an \(O(d\log T)\) guarantee would still require substantial analytical work.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The original open problem asks for a *practical* (polynomial-time, integration-free) online portfolio selection algorithm on \(\Delta_d\) with worst-case regret \(O(d\log T)\) against the best constantly rebalanced portfolio under log loss \(\ell_t(w)=-\log(x_t^\top w)\), matching Cover’s Universal Portfolios rate \((d-1)\log(T+1)\) but without computing the high-dimensional posterior mean \(\int_{\Delta_d} w\,\phi_t(w)\,dw\). The 2025 paper "Efficient and Near-Optimal Online Portfolio Selection" itself proposes VB-FTRL, which replaces the intractable Bayesian averaging step by a per-round convex minimization of a *hybrid logarithmic–volumetric barrier* potential \(P_t(w)=\sum_{\tau\le t}\ell_\tau(w)+\lambda R(w)+\mu V_t(w)\), where \(R\) is the simplex log barrier and \(V_t(w)=\tfrac12\log\det(A^\top\nabla^2 L_t(w)A)\) is the volumetric barrier built from the Hessian of the regularized cumulative loss. This yields regret \(O(d\log(T+d))\) with an efficient Newton/quasi-Newton implementation costing about \(\tilde O(d^2(T+d))\) per round, thereby meeting the stated goal up to the benign replacement \(\log T\mapsto \log(T+d)\) and constant factors.

Subsequent and related works largely develop complementary efficient second-order and barrier-based techniques, but (as reflected in the forward-citation summaries provided) they typically either (i) achieve \(\tilde O(d\log T)\) regret only under additional boundedness/Lipschitz assumptions (e.g. general exp-concave OCO frameworks such as "Quasi-newton steps for efficient online exp-concave optimization"), or (ii) obtain \(\tilde O(\sqrt{dT})\) worst-case regret while being highly practical and robust to non-Lipschitzness (e.g. BARONS and log-barrier optimistic-FTRL lines). The quantum generalization "Online learning quantum states with the logarithmic loss via VB-FTRL" underscores the broader reach of volumetric-barrier regularization and variational interpretations of universal mixtures, but does not improve the classical worst-case rate beyond VB-FTRL.

Overall, in light of VB-FTRL, the specific computational open problem as posed in the source paper is best viewed as *resolved in the affirmative* at the level of worst-case order-optimal regret with a polynomial-time, integration-free update; remaining gaps concern sharpening constants, reducing the \(\tilde O(d^2T)\) per-round dependence on the horizon in the implementation, and understanding whether one can match the exact \((d-1)\log(T+1)\) constant (or remove the \(+d\) inside the logarithm) with similarly practical updates under fully adversarial nonnegative returns.

sources.title: Online learning quantum states with the logarithmic loss via VB-FTRL
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves (Theorem 1.1 / Theorem 3.1) that a generalized VB-FTRL algorithm for online learning of quantum states with logarithmic loss achieves regret \(O(d^2\log(d+T))\). Each round computes \(\rho_t\in\arg\min_{\rho\in\mathcal D_d} P_{t-1}(\rho)\) where \(P_t(\rho)=\sum_{\tau=1}^t -\log\operatorname{tr}(A_\tau\rho)+\lambda(-\log\det\rho)+\mu\,\tfrac12\log\det\nabla^2 L_t(\mathrm{vec}(\rho))\). They introduce “VB-convexity” and show (Theorem 2.5/Corollary 2.6) that if \(\varphi\) is VB-convex then \(\mathrm{VB}_\varphi(x)=\tfrac12\log\det\nabla^2\varphi(x)\) is convex, enabling convex optimization each round (via SDP/cutting-plane).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a practical, per-round polynomial-time OPS algorithm with regret \(O(d\log T)\) without high-dimensional integration. This paper is primarily about the quantum generalization (LL-OLQS), but it explicitly builds on VB-FTRL (Jze9quel et al.), which is a candidate solution to the classical OPS open problem. The authors note that when all matrices commute (share an eigenbasis), their algorithm specializes to an OPS algorithm; however, the regret bound they prove in the quantum setting is \(O(d^2\log(d+T))\), and they do not establish an \(O(d\log T)\) regret guarantee for the classical OPS specialization nor provide a near-quadratic-in-\(d\) implementation for OPS. Their implementation discussion uses cutting-plane/ellipsoid methods with very high complexity (e.g. \(O(T d^8 + d^{10})\) per round for the quantum case), so it does not directly meet the ‘practical’ efficiency target of the OPS open problem.

sources.title: Quasi-newton steps for efficient online exp-concave optimization
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper gives a projection-free quasi-Newton algorithm for online exp-concave optimization that achieves near-optimal regret with improved runtime. In particular, for -exp-concave, 1-Lipschitz losses over a convex set \(C\subseteq B(1)\), Algorithm 2 (reduction to the ball) with subroutine Algorithm 1 (OQNS) guarantees regret \(\sum_{t=1}^T (f_t(u_t)-f_t(w)) = O\big(d(\alpha^{-1}+G)\ln(dT)\big)\) for all \(w\in C\) (Theorem 10), with total time \(\tilde O((T_{\mathrm{proj}}(C)+d^2)T + d^\omega \sqrt T)\) (or \(\tilde O((T_{\mathrm{sep}}(C)+d^2)T + d^\omega \sqrt T)\) for centrally symmetric \(C\)). For the ball subproblem, Algorithm 1 achieves surrogate/actual regret \(O(d\ln(d+T))\) while computing a full matrix inverse only \(\tilde O(\sqrt T)\) times, yielding total time \(\tilde O(d^2 T + d^\omega \sqrt T)\) (Theorem 7 and Lemma 4).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a practical, computationally feasible online portfolio selection algorithm with worst-case regret \(O(d\log T)\) for Cover's log loss \(\ell_t(w)=-\log(x_t^\top w)\) on the simplex, avoiding the high-dimensional integration of Universal Portfolios. This paper does not directly instantiate an algorithm specialized to the simplex/log-loss portfolio game, but it develops a general efficient framework for online exp-concave optimization with \(\tilde O(d^2)\) amortized per-round cost and \(O(d\log T)\)-type regret. Since Cover's loss is exp-concave on the simplex (under standard boundedness assumptions ensuring Lipschitz gradients), their methods are conceptually relevant: one could apply their general algorithm to \(C=\Delta_d\) (via the reduction to a ball, requiring Euclidean projection onto the simplex, which is efficient) to obtain \(O(d\log T)\) regret with per-round time roughly \(\tilde O(d^2 + T_{\mathrm{proj}}(\Delta_d))\). However, the regret bound in Theorem 10 is stated as \(O(d(\alpha^{-1}+G)\ln(dT))\) for Lipschitz exp-concave losses, not explicitly as the sharp \((d-1)\log(T+1)\) constant of Universal Portfolios, and the portfolio setting may require additional assumptions (e.g. bounded returns away from 0) to meet Lipschitz/gradient bounds. Thus it provides partial progress/techniques toward the open problem rather than a complete resolution as stated for all nonnegative returns.

sources.title: Projection-free online convex optimization via efficient Newton iterations
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proposes BARONS (Barrier-Regularized Online Newton Step), a projection-free OCO algorithm using a self-concordant barrier 

- Generic regret (Theorem 5): if 
\(\|g_t\|_{\nabla^{-2}\Phi(w_t)}\le b\) and parameters satisfy \(\eta\le 1/(1000 b M_\Phi)\), \(\varepsilon\le 1/(20000 M_\Phi)\), then for all \(w\in\mathrm{int}(K)\),
\[\sum_{t=1}^T (\ell_t(w_t)-\ell_t(w)) \lesssim \tfrac{1}{\eta}\Phi(w)+\eta\sum_{t=1}^T \|g_t\|_{\nabla^{-2}\Phi(w_t)}^2+\varepsilon\sum_{t=1}^T \|g_t\|_{\nabla^{-2}\Phi(w_t)}.\]
- With an \((M_\Phi,\nu)\)-self-concordant barrier and local-norm bound, BARONS achieves \(\mathrm{Reg}_T\lesssim b\sqrt{\nu T\log(1/c)}\) against comparators in a shrunken set \(K_c\) (Theorem 6).
- For polytopes using the Lee
Sidford barrier, \(\nu=\tilde O(d)\) giving \(\mathrm{Reg}_T\lesssim b\sqrt{dT\log(1/c)}\) (Corollary 1).
- For online portfolio selection on \(\Delta_d\) with log-loss \(\ell_t(w)=-\log(r_t^\top w)\), instantiating BARONS with the simplex log-barrier yields \(\tilde O(\sqrt{dT})\) regret with total time essentially \(O(d^2T)\) (discussion in
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for a computationally feasible online portfolio selection algorithm achieving worst-case regret \(O(d\log T)\) (matching Universal Portfolios) without high-dimensional integration. This paper is relevant because it develops a computationally efficient barrier-based online Newton method (BARONS) that avoids projections and, when applied to the portfolio selection loss \(\ell_t(w)=-\log(r_t^\top w)\) on the simplex, yields an efficient update (claimed essentially \(O(d^2)\) per round) without any integration/sampling from Cover's posterior.
However, the regret guarantee they derive for portfolio selection is only \(\tilde O(\sqrt{dT})\), not \(O(d\log T)\). Thus it does not solve the stated open problem; it provides an efficient alternative with sublinear but much larger (in \(T\)) regret, and contributes techniques (amortized Hessian/inverse maintenance for barrier Newton steps) that could be relevant for designing practical algorithms.

sources.title: Data-dependent bounds for online portfolio selection without lipschitzness and smoothness
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper gives new log-barrier FTRL-type algorithms for online portfolio selection (log loss \,\(f_t(x)=-\log\langle a_t,x\rangle\) on \(\Delta_d\)) with per-round time \(\tilde O(d)\) and data-dependent regret guarantees without the no-junk-bonds assumption. In particular:
- (Theorem 6.1) Algorithm 3 achieves \(R_T(x) \le (\log T+8)\sqrt{d V_T + 512 d^2} + \sqrt{2}d\log T + O(d)\), where \(V_T=\sum_{t=2}^T \|\nabla f_t(x_{t-1})-\nabla f_{t-1}(x_{t-1})\|_{x_{t-1},*}^2\). This yields worst-case \(\tilde O(\sqrt{dT})\) regret and best-case \(O(d\log T)\) when \(V_T=0\).
- (Theorem 6.2) Algorithm 4 achieves a small-loss bound \(R_T \le 2(\log T+2)\sqrt{4d L_T^\star + 4d^2 + d + d(\log T+2)^2}\), where \(L_T^\star=\min_{x\in\Delta_d}\sum_{t=1}^T f_t(x)\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a computationally feasible algorithm achieving worst-case regret \(O(d\log T)\) (matching Universal Portfolios) without high-dimensional integration. This paper provides computationally efficient (\(\tilde O(d)\) per round) log-barrier optimistic-FTRL algorithms that avoid integration, but their worst-case regret remains \(\tilde O(\sqrt{dT})\), not \(O(d\log T)\). They do achieve \(O(d\,\mathrm{polylog}(T))\) regret in certain “easy data” regimes (e.g. constant price relatives or \(L_T^\star=0\)), so they constitute partial progress toward practicality and understanding, but do not resolve the worst-case optimal regret-efficiency tradeoff posed in the open problem.

sources.title: Data Dependent Regret Bounds for Online Portfolio Selection with Predicted Returns
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves data-dependent regret bounds for online portfolio selection (log loss) without the no-junk-bonds assumption using log-barrier optimistic FTRL variants computable in near-linear time per round. In particular:
- (Gradual-variation) Algorithm 3 satisfies for all horizons and sequences (under normalization \(\|x_t\|_\infty=1\))
\[R_T \le (\log d+8)\sqrt{V_T+512}+\sqrt{2}\log d+2-128\sqrt{2},\]
where \(V_T=\sum_{t=2}^T \|\nabla \ell_{t-1}(w_{t-1})-\nabla \ell_t(w_{t-1})\|_{t-1,*}^2\) is a local-norm gradual-variation quantity.
- (Small-loss) Algorithm 4 satisfies
\[R_T \le 2(\log d+2)\sqrt{4L_T^*+4\log^2 d+\log d}+(\log d+2)^2,\]
where \(L_T^*=\min_{w\in\Delta_d}\sum_{t=1}^T \ell_t(w).\)
Both algorithms have per-round time \(\tilde O(d)\) (solving a 1D self-concordant Newton subproblem).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a practical algorithm achieving worst-case regret \(O(d\log T)\) (universal-portfolio optimal) with polynomial-time per-round updates avoiding high-dimensional integration. This paper explicitly targets computational efficiency (\(\tilde O(d)\) per round) and avoids high-dimensional integration by using log-barrier optimistic FTRL with a 1D Newton solve. However, its worst-case regret guarantees are \(\tilde O(\sqrt{T})\) (Algorithms 3/4) rather than \(O(d\log T)\). Thus it does not solve the open problem, but provides partial progress: efficient algorithms with improved data-dependent (easy-data) logarithmic-in-\(T\) behavior while retaining sublinear worst-case regret without the no-junk-bonds assumption.

sources.title: Data-dependent Regret Bounds for Adversarial Multi-Armed Bandits and Online Portfolio Selection
sources.theorem_or_result: The dissertation proposes OPS algorithms AdaCurv ONS and LB-AdaCurv ONS for log-loss \,\(f_t(w)=-\log(r_t^\top w)\) on the simplex. It proves (Theorem 4.2) a data-dependent bound for AdaCurv ONS: \(\sum_{t=1}^T f_t(w_t)-f_t(w)\le \tfrac12+\tfrac{nR}{2}\log(1+TR)\) (for a particular choice of parameters), where \(R=\max_{t,i,j} r_t(i)/r_t(j)\). It also proves (Theorem 4.3) for LB-AdaCurv ONS: \(\sum_{t=1}^T f_t(w_t)-f_t(w)\le \tfrac52+2n\log T+\min\{nR\log(1+RT),\;2+2\sqrt{2nT\log T}\}\), giving worst-case \(O(\sqrt{nT\log T})\) while retaining \(O(nR\log T)\) when \(R\) is bounded.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a computationally feasible OPS algorithm achieving worst-case regret \(O(d\log T)\) for all nonnegative return sequences, matching Universal Portfolios, without high-dimensional integration. This dissertation explicitly discusses that open problem and gives efficient (per-round polynomial-time, e.g. \(O(n^3)\)) second-order/FTRL-style algorithms avoiding integration. However, the proved worst-case guarantee for the proposed robust algorithm LB-AdaCurv ONS is \(O(\sqrt{nT\log T})\), not \(O(n\log T)\) uniformly over all sequences. The \(O(nR\log T)\) bound matches \(O(n\log T)\) only when the data-dependent ratio \(R\) is \(O(1)\) (e.g. bounded returns away from 0), which is not assumed in the open problem (returns merely nonnegative). Thus it provides partial progress (efficient algorithms + optimal regret under additional boundedness/benignness conditions), but does not resolve the minimax \(\Theta(n\log T)\) worst-case objective in full generality.
